abstract:
Bisphenol A (BPA) is a ubiquitous endocrine disruptor found in a wide array of everyday consumer products. In response to mounting safety concerns and increasingly stringent legislative restrictions, the industry has transitioned toward using structural analogues that possess similar polymer-forming properties but remain far less understood. Growing evidence suggests that many of these substitutes mirror the harmful effects of BPA, disrupting endocrine signaling through agonistic or antagonistic actions at various nuclear receptors, including ER, AR, GR, AhR, and PXR.

Recently, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) drastically lowered the temporary tolerable daily intake (t-TDI) of BPA from 4 µg/kg to 0.2 ng/kg of body weight per day, citing its potential to compromise immune system integrity. Motivated by this shift in the regulatory landscape, we conducted a comprehensive review of the immunomodulatory potential of environmentally prevalent BPA analogues. Our findings indicate that these substitutes can interfere with both innate and adaptive immune responses, potentially contributing to the development of hypersensitivity disorders, allergies, and significant alterations in the human microbiome.
